 Demystifying Apple Pay 

## Demystifying Apple Pay

 
 A Payment PM's Guide to What It Actually Takes to Deploy 'Just Tap and Go' 
 
 Kiran · 19 December 2024 · 11 min read · Demystified Apple Pay Digital Wallets Paze Payment Architecture Tokenization Fintech Product Strategy 
 
 
 
 
 2014, Wells Fargo headquarters. I'm watching the Apple Pay launch event on screens whilst simultaneously fielding calls from my engineering team about token provisioning failures. Tim Cook makes it look effortless on stage - just tap and go. Meanwhile, I'm debugging why certain card types aren't generating tokens properly. The disconnect between the stage magic and the backend complexity hits me: customers will never see the months of integration work, the security protocols, the fail-safes I've built. They'll just expect it to work. 
 
 That's the thing about great payment experiences - the harder they are to build, the more invisible they should be. Nearly a decade later, Apple Pay has become so seamless that most payments product managers take its architecture for granted. But for anyone deploying payment experiences, the architecture beneath the magic is the difference between shipping with confidence and shipping with crossed fingers. 
 
 This isn't about appreciating Apple's engineering prowess. It's about recognizing how they solved problems that every payments PM faces: security without friction, platform control without custody risk, and ecosystem growth without compromising user trust. The solutions they chose reveal principles that apply far beyond iOS wallets. 
 The Architecture That Makes It Work 

## The Architecture That Makes It Work

 
 Apple Pay succeeds because it distributes complexity across an entire ecosystem - from the chip in your phone to the issuing bank that approves the charge. The diagram below maps the full transaction architecture: five layers, five steps, and a return path that completes the loop. Most payment systems try to centralise control. Apple deliberately fragments responsibility across every layer. 
 
 
 
 Let me walk through each layer the way I wish someone had explained it to me in 2014 - as a bank PM trying to understand what my team actually needed to build, what Apple controlled, and what existing infrastructure we could lean on. 
 
 Device Layer: Where the Security Lives 
 Start at the top of the diagram. The iPhone contains three components that matter: the Secure Element (SE), a tamper-resistant chip isolated from iOS that stores payment tokens and cryptographic keys; the Wallet app and iOS framework that orchestrate the user experience and biometric authentication; and the NFC controller that handles the contactless data exchange. 
 
 From a bank's perspective, the SE is the critical piece. It's a tiny, isolated computer that can perform calculations but never reveals its contents. Even if someone compromises the phone's operating system, they can't access the payment data. For us at Wells Fargo, this meant we could trust the device with tokenized credentials without worrying about a software vulnerability exposing real card numbers. Apple deliberately can't see them either - which is what convinced our security team. 
 
 The dashed callout box on the right side of the diagram matters: the device stores a Device Account Number (a token that maps to the real card), cryptographic keys (locked inside the SE), and biometric data. What it never stores is the actual card number. That separation is what makes the whole system work. 
 
 Merchant Layer: What Changes (and What Doesn't) at the Terminal 
 Step 2 in the diagram - the NFC tap. When a customer holds their phone to a terminal, the merchant's POS system receives a tokenized payment. From the merchant's perspective, it looks like a regular card transaction. They see only a token and a one-time cryptogram, both useless if intercepted. 
 
 This was Apple's smartest architectural decision for adoption. Merchants didn't need new terminals, new settlement relationships, or new software. If their terminal already accepted contactless cards, it accepted Apple Pay. For a bank PM evaluating the integration, this meant we didn't need to coordinate with merchant acquirers or convince retailers to upgrade. The merchant layer was essentially invisible to our implementation. 
 
 Network Layer: Riding Existing Rails 
 Step 3 follows established card network infrastructure. The tokenized payment flows from the merchant's acquirer through Visa, Mastercard, or Amex - the same rails that handle every other card transaction. The Token Service Provider (usually operated by the card network itself) sits alongside and maintains the mapping between tokens and real card numbers. 
 
 For a bank considering Apple Pay deployment, this is the layer that keeps the integration manageable. You're not building new connections to a novel payment network. You're plugging into the same Visa and Mastercard infrastructure your bank already uses. The incremental work is in token provisioning and de-tokenisation - not in rearchitecting your payment flows. 
 
 Issuing Bank Layer: Where the Bank Does Its Real Work 
 Step 4 is where the transaction lands on the bank's side. The issuing bank's token vault translates the token back to the real card number, verifies the cryptographic signature, and passes it to the authorization and fraud engine. This is the layer where I spent most of my time in 2014 - the balance checks, risk scoring, and fraud models that decide approve or decline. 
 
 The return path (Step 5 on the diagram's right edge) sends that decision back through the same card network rails to the merchant terminal and ultimately back to the customer's device. The entire round trip takes under a second, but every layer has done its job. 
 
 What matters from the bank PM's seat: your existing auth and fraud engines work without modification. The token vault is the new piece - a system that maps tokens to real PANs and validates that the cryptographic signatures match. That's the core integration work. 
 
 Apple Cloud Layer: The Provisioning Engine 
 The bottom of the diagram shows a layer that's easy to overlook because it's not involved in payments at all. Apple's cloud services handle card setup (when a customer first adds their card to Wallet), device management (syncing tokens across iPhone, Watch, and iPad), and remote wipe (disabling payment credentials if a device is lost). 
 
 For a bank deploying Apple Pay, this is where the initial partnership friction lives. Your bank needs to integrate with Apple's provisioning service to verify cardholders, approve token creation, and manage the lifecycle. Once that's running, the payment flow itself rides entirely on existing infrastructure - the cloud layer steps aside. 
 
 This architecture means compromise of any single layer doesn't expose the entire system. It's defense in depth, but architected for usability rather than paranoia. And for a bank PM scoping the integration, it means the work is concentrated in two places: the token vault (new) and the provisioning integration with Apple's cloud (new). Everything else - your terminals, your card networks, your auth engines - keeps working as before. 
 What Happens in Under a Second 

## What Happens in Under a Second

 
 The diagram shows the layers. Here's what the timing actually looks like when a customer taps their phone. 
 
 Double-click, Face ID, tap. In that gesture, the Secure Element unlocks, the NFC controller negotiates with the terminal, and a one-time token with a dynamic cryptogram is generated and transmitted - all before the customer's thumb leaves the phone. The terminal forwards the tokenized payment through the card network's existing rails. The issuing bank's token vault translates it back to a real account, the fraud engine scores it, and an approve or decline decision travels back through the same path. The entire round trip - device to bank and back - completes in under a second. 
 
 The thing worth appreciating is what doesn't happen. The merchant never sees the real card number. The SE creates a payment credential that proves the customer owns a valid card without revealing what that card is. Even if someone intercepts the data mid-flight, they can't reverse-engineer the actual account details or reuse the cryptogram for another transaction. 
 
 And settlement? It flows through the same networks that handle regular card transactions. The merchant receives funds just as they would for any Visa or Mastercard payment. From their perspective, Apple Pay is simply another card-present transaction. The complexity is entirely hidden, which is exactly why adoption didn't require merchants or banks to rearchitect their financial plumbing. 
 The Business Model Behind the 'Free' Wallet 

## The Business Model Behind the 'Free' Wallet

 
 Apple Pay appears free to users, but that's because the economic model runs on different rails. Understanding the revenue streams reveals why Apple chose this architecture over alternatives. 
 
 Revenue from Card Networks and Banks 
 According to industry estimates, Apple collects approximately 0.15% of every Apple Pay transaction from card networks, with banks absorbing most of this cost. For a £100 purchase, Apple earns about 15 pence. This might seem modest, but consider the scale: Apple Pay processed over $6 trillion in transactions in 2023, according to company reports. Even tiny percentages become substantial revenue streams. 
 
 The strategic insight: Apple chose recurring, usage-based revenue over higher-margin but lower-volume alternatives. They could have built their own payment network or charged merchants directly, but both approaches would have required building new financial infrastructure and convincing new stakeholders. Instead, they inserted themselves into existing value chains and extracted ongoing fees from willing partners. 
 
 Platform Lock-in and Services Revenue 
 The deeper play isn't the transaction fees - it's strengthening iOS ecosystem stickiness. Once you've configured Apple Pay with multiple cards, switching to Android means rebuilding your entire digital wallet. The switching cost isn't financial; it's operational friction. 
 
 This explains why Apple hasn't aggressively monetised Apple Pay features that could generate higher short-term revenue. They're optimising for long-term platform retention rather than immediate wallet profitability. 
 
 Strategic Positioning vs. Competitive Threats 
 By 2014, Google Wallet and PayPal were positioning themselves as intermediaries between consumers and their banks. Apple chose the opposite strategy: disappear from the financial relationship whilst controlling the user experience. Banks preferred this approach because Apple didn't threaten their customer relationships or try to own the billing relationship. 
 What's Changed Since Wells Fargo's 2014 Launch 

## What's Changed Since Wells Fargo's 2014 Launch

 
 The core architecture remains remarkably stable, but the platform has evolved in ways that reveal Apple's broader ambitions. 
 
 Beyond Payments: Identity and Access 
 Apple Pay now stores driver's licences, student IDs, and transit cards alongside payment credentials. The same Secure Element architecture that protects financial data now manages broader identity credentials. This expansion transforms Apple Pay from a payment method into a comprehensive digital identity platform. 
 
 The technical infrastructure was always capable of this - it just took time to negotiate with institutions that issue identity credentials. The payment partnerships created the trust foundation that enabled these broader use cases. 
 
 Enhanced Security and Privacy Features 
 Apple introduced features like transaction-specific dynamic security codes and enhanced fraud detection that weren't possible in 2014. The Secure Element's computing power has increased, enabling more sophisticated cryptographic operations whilst maintaining the same security boundaries. 
 
 More importantly, Apple has used their scale to push privacy standards across the industry. The success of Apple Pay's privacy model influenced how other platforms approach financial data custody. 
 
 Ecosystem Expansion 
 Apple Pay now works across Apple Watch, iPad, and Mac, with each device requiring separate security provisioning. The cloud layer manages this multi-device complexity whilst maintaining the principle that payment credentials stay hardware-bound. 
 
 The infrastructure built for simple iPhone payments now supports a comprehensive ecosystem of connected devices, each with different interaction models but consistent security standards. 
 
 The Paze Experiment: What Happens When Banks Try to Build Their Own 
 In 2023, seven of the largest US banks - including Wells Fargo, JPMorgan Chase, and Bank of America - launched Paze, a digital wallet built through Early Warning Services (the same consortium behind Zelle). The motivation was straightforward: Apple collects roughly 0.15% of every Apple Pay transaction from card networks, with banks absorbing most of that cost. At Apple Pay's scale (over $6 trillion in transactions in 2023, per company reports), even that thin margin adds up to projected revenues approaching $1 billion globally. Banks wanted to reclaim that value and reduce their dependence on a platform they didn't control. 
 
 The strategy seemed sound on paper. Paze reported pre-loading some 150 million debit and credit card accounts from its member banks. It targeted e-commerce checkout (not in-store), where it didn't need to compete with Apple's NFC hardware advantage. And it leveraged the trust consumers already had in their banks. 
 
 But two years in, the results tell a different story about Apple Pay's stickiness. Paze has struggled with consumer awareness and active adoption, despite the sheer number of cards loaded onto the system. Industry analysts have questioned what would motivate someone already using Apple Pay to switch. The rollout took longer than planned, missing its initial 2023 holiday season target. And the fundamental challenge remains: Paze is e-commerce only, whilst Apple Pay works everywhere - in-store, online, and in-app. 
 
 What Paze reveals about Apple Pay's architecture is more interesting than Paze itself. The diagram above shows why switching is so hard. Apple Pay isn't just a software layer sitting on top of existing rails - it's embedded in device hardware (the Secure Element), woven into the OS (biometric authentication), and backed by cloud provisioning that syncs across every Apple device you own. To replicate that experience, you'd need to control the hardware, the operating system, and the cloud infrastructure. A bank-owned e-commerce wallet, no matter how well-funded, can't recreate that vertical integration. 
 
 The deeper lesson for payments PMs: the 0.15% fee Apple charges isn't really a fee for processing payments. It's a fee for owning the most frictionless authentication and trust layer in consumer payments. Banks built Paze to avoid paying that fee. What they discovered is that the fee buys something they can't easily build themselves - an experience so seamless that users don't want to leave it. 
 The Strategic Questions Every Payments PM Should Ask 

## The Strategic Questions Every Payments PM Should Ask

 
 Apple Pay's architecture reveals five principles that apply beyond iOS wallets: 
 
 Can you distribute complexity instead of centralising control? The most secure systems often fragment responsibility across multiple layers that can't compromise each other. 
 
 Are you building on existing rails or creating new ones? Apple succeeded partly because they innovated selectively - enhancing user experience and security whilst leveraging existing financial infrastructure. 
 
 What's your stance on data custody? Apple's "we can't see your data" positioning became a competitive advantage. Sometimes the most powerful architecture choice is deliberate blindness. 
 
 How do you balance platform control with partner trust? Apple maintained strict control over user experience whilst reassuring banks they wouldn't be disintermediated. 
 
 What switching costs are you creating? The most durable platforms create operational friction for leaving, not financial penalties. 
 
 That 2014 launch day complexity has become invisible infrastructure that processes trillions annually. The real lesson isn't about the technology - it's about architecting complexity in service of simplicity. The best payment experiences feel inevitable in retrospect, but they require deliberate choices about where to hide the machinery. 
 
 For payments PMs, understanding these architectural decisions isn't about copying Apple's approach. It's about recognizing the principles behind choices that seem obvious now but were genuinely uncertain when they mattered most.

 The Bank That Got 213,000 Employees to Actually Use AI - By Not Calling It AI 

## The Bank That Got 213,000 Employees to Actually Use AI - By Not Calling It AI

 
 Kiran · 17 February 2026 · 7 min read · Case Study AI Change Management 
 
 
 
 The Password Reset That Changed Everything 

## The Password Reset That Changed Everything

 
 In the spring of 2020, while most of the world was figuring out how to unmute themselves on Zoom, a team at Bank of America quietly launched an internal chatbot. Its job was unglamorous: help employees reset their passwords and activate devices. 
 
 Nobody called it artificial intelligence. Nobody sent an all-hands email about digital transformation. Nobody ran a training programme on "the future of work". They just gave people a tool that solved an annoying problem - and then watched what happened. 
 
 Today, over 90% of Bank of America's 213,000 employees use that tool. IT helpdesk calls have dropped by more than 50%. And the bank has become, almost by accident, one of the most successful cases of enterprise AI adoption in the world. 
 
 The story of how that happened is not the story most organisations are telling themselves about AI. It's quieter, less glamorous, and far more instructive. 
 The Resistance Problem Nobody Wants to Admit 

## The Resistance Problem Nobody Wants to Admit

 
 Here's the uncomfortable truth about enterprise AI in 2025: almost none of it is working. 
 
 A July 2025 report from MIT's Project NANDA found that 95% of generative AI pilots fail to deliver measurable business impact. Not 50%. Not 70%. Ninety-five per cent. Despite $30-40 billion in enterprise investment, only 5% of integrated AI pilots are extracting meaningful value. 
 
 And the problem isn't the technology. It's the people - or more precisely, it's how organisations are treating the people. 
 
 A 2025 survey by Writer found that 41% of millennial and Gen Z workers have actively sabotaged or circumvented AI tools at work. Not because they're technophobes - this is the generation that grew up with smartphones. They're resisting because they feel threatened. When the CEO announces an AI transformation while the headlines scream about job cuts, every new tool feels less like an upgrade and more like an audition for your own replacement. 
 
 This is the gap that most AI strategies fall into: leadership sees efficiency, employees see existential risk, and the technology sits in the middle, unused. 
 Start With the Problem, Not the Technology 

## Start With the Problem, Not the Technology

 
 Bank of America didn't fall into that gap - largely because they never tried to jump over it. 
 
 When the bank launched Erica for Employees in 2020, it wasn't positioned as an AI initiative. It was positioned as a better way to get your password reset without sitting on hold for 45 minutes. During the chaos of the pandemic, with hundreds of thousands of employees suddenly remote and IT helpdesks overwhelmed, that mattered. 
 
 The tool worked. People used it. And then something interesting happened: the bank expanded it. In 2023, Erica for Employees grew to handle benefits enquiries, payroll questions, and tax form requests. Each expansion followed the same logic - find a specific, real pain point that employees already have, and solve it. Not "reimagine the employee experience". Not "leverage AI to drive operational excellence". Just solve the problem. 
 
 By 2025, the platform had become something employees reached for instinctively. Not because they were told to. Because it was genuinely useful. 
 
 This is what most AI rollouts get backwards. They start with the technology and go looking for problems to solve. Bank of America started with the problems and let the technology earn its place. 
 The Manager Multiplier 

## The Manager Multiplier

 
 I saw this dynamic play out on my own team last year. We'd rolled out an AI tool for document review - solid tool, good training, executive sponsorship, the works. Adoption was crawling. Then one of my team leads - completely unprompted - dropped a message in our team Slack: "Just used this to pull together the compliance summary for Thursday's review. Took me 12 minutes instead of the usual two hours. Sharing my prompt in case it's useful." 
 
 Within a week, half the team was using it. No training session accomplished what that one Slack message did. 
 
 This shouldn't have surprised me, but it did. And the data explains why it works. Gallup's Fall 2025 workforce survey found that employees whose managers actively support AI use are more than twice as likely to use it frequently. These employees are also far more likely to believe AI tools actually help them perform well. Yet only about 30% of workers say their manager provides this kind of support. 
 
 That's a staggering gap - and it points to why so many AI rollouts stall. Organisations invest in the technology, invest in executive sponsorship, invest in training programmes - and then ignore the people who actually shape how teams work day to day. 
 
 The manager isn't a bottleneck. The manager is the distribution layer. When a team leader shares a prompt that saved them two hours, that's worth more than any corporate training video. When a manager helps a direct report figure out how a tool fits into their specific workflow, that's adoption happening in real time. 
 
 Bank of America understood this instinctively. As Aditya Bhasin, the bank's chief technology and information officer, put it, the focus was on making AI a tool for "employee efficiency and operational excellence" - not a mandate from above, but a capability that managers could champion within their teams. 
 What Happens When You Cut Before You Convert 

## What Happens When You Cut Before You Convert

 
 Not every bank took this approach. And the contrast is revealing. 
 
 In February 2025, DBS Group - Southeast Asia's largest bank - announced it would cut 4,000 temporary and contract roles over three years as AI became embedded in operations. Outgoing CEO Piyush Gupta told an industry conference that he was, "for the first time, struggling to create jobs". 
 
 Now, DBS's AI programme is impressive by any measure. The bank runs over 370 AI use cases across 1,500 models, and incoming CEO Tan Su Shan told CNBC in November 2025 that AI was already delivering a revenue bump of more than SG$1 billion. This is not a company playing at AI. This is real, measurable, significant value. 
 
 But there's a tension. When 4,000 job cuts make the headlines alongside an AI rollout, every remaining employee reads the subtext. No amount of upskilling programmes - and DBS has earmarked 13,000 staff for retraining - fully erases the message that the technology is here to replace you. 
 
 JPMorgan sits somewhere in between. The bank rolled out its LLM Suite to more than 200,000 employees, and CEO Jamie Dimon said at Davos in January 2026 that AI's effect on the labour market "may go too fast for society". Productivity in areas using AI reportedly rose from 3% to 6%, with projections of 40-50% gains in operations. But the bank has also projected a 10% decline in operations staff as agentic AI takes over more complex tasks. 
 
 Here's the thing that gets lost in these stories: you cannot get people to identify customer value if all you have is leadership and technology. The employees who understand the edge cases, who know which customer complaints signal a deeper problem, who spot the workarounds that keep processes running - those are the people who make AI work in practice. Cut them before they've had the chance to become AI-literate, and you're left with powerful tools and nobody who knows what to do with them. 
 The Metric That Actually Matters 

## The Metric That Actually Matters

 
 There's a deeper lesson embedded in Bank of America's numbers, and it has to do with what you choose to measure. 
 
 Most organisations measure AI deployment - tools launched, licences purchased, models trained. Bank of America measures adoption. The 90% figure isn't about how many employees have access to Erica for Employees. It's about how many actually use it. Regularly. Voluntarily. 
 
 That distinction matters enormously. An IBM study of 297 banking and financial markets CEOs found that 65% believe success with AI will depend more on people's adoption than the technology itself. And yet, in the same study, 60% admitted they were pushing AI adoption faster than some employees found comfortable. 
 
 That tension - knowing that people matter most, but moving too fast for the people - is the central contradiction of enterprise AI in 2026. The organisations that resolve it will be the ones that measure what matters: not how much AI they've deployed, but how much their people trust it. 
 The Unglamorous Truth About Transformation 

## The Unglamorous Truth About Transformation

 
 Bank of America's story isn't the kind that wins innovation awards. There's no dramatic pivot, no billion-dollar AI lab, no visionary CEO speech about the future. There's a password reset tool that got better, a team lead who shared a Slack message, and 213,000 people who use AI every day because it makes their work a little less painful. 
 
 That's the unglamorous truth about transformation: the organisations that actually get there are rarely the ones that talk about it the loudest. They're the ones that start small, earn trust, and never forget that technology without people who believe in it is just expensive infrastructure. 
 
 Ninety-five per cent of AI pilots fail. Bank of America landed in the other five. Not because they had better technology. Because they invested in people first. 
 
 Sources 

## Sources

 MIT Project NANDA, "The GenAI Divide: State of AI in Business 2025", July 2025 - 95% of GenAI pilots fail to deliver measurable business impact; only 5% reach production with P&L impact. 
 
 Writer, "2025 Enterprise AI Report", 2025 - 41% of millennial and Gen Z workers have sabotaged or circumvented AI tools at work. 
 
 Gallup, "Manager Support Drives Employee AI Adoption", Fall 2025 - Employees with manager support are 2x more likely to use AI frequently; only ~30% of workers report receiving this support.

## Career Highlights

 
 15+ years. The best proof isn't a resume - it's the work. Here's mine. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Each card below has details of the initiatives I led. Open the cards to read more about what I did and why. If you want to know more about how I work - try asking Fenix. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Most commonly asked questions about Kiran's career ↘

 Try asking Fenix ↘

## VP of Product

 
 Avatour AI - AR/VR Startup · Oct 2025 - Present

 AI 0 to 1 AR/VR Startup 
 
 Initiatives 
 
 
 
 
 ⚙ 
 
 
 AI-Assisted Reporting Platform 
 
 Pivoted core product from live inspections to async AI-powered reporting 
 
 
 3.2x TAM 
 
 
 
 
 AI Strategy 0 to 1 
 
 
 
 The Problem 
 
 Avatour's core product was built for live remote inspections via 360 video. But the market for real-time walkthroughs was niche - limited TAM, long sales cycles, and heavy reliance on synchronous scheduling across time zones. 
 
 
 
 The Bet 
 
 If we shifted from live-only to async AI-assisted reporting - where a single site visit generates structured, shareable inspection reports - we could serve a much larger market without requiring both parties to be online at the same time. 
 
 
 
 What Shipped 
 
 AI summarization of 360 captures into structured reports. Auto-tagging of defects and observations. Conversational AI agent for report Q&A, reducing back-and-forth between field teams and reviewers. 
 
 
 
 The Outcome 
 
 Expanded TAM by 3.2x to $45M. Reduced reporting time by 80%. Conversational agent cut support load by 37%. Unlocked new verticals (insurance, construction, facilities) that were previously impractical.

 The work below represents years past of building alongside teams I respect at companies that trusted me with meaningful problems. Some of it involved strategic work that requires care in how I share it. I've gated the detail behind a simple access code out of respect for those organizations. I'm proud to share my work with the right people for the right reasons. Reach out on LinkedIn for a code. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 VP of Product, Mobile & AI Growth 

## VP of Product, Mobile & AI Growth

 
 Wells Fargo - Strategy, Digital & AI · Nov 2023 - Oct 2025 
 
 
 
 Expanded consumer mobile app base 18M to 32M ; JD Power #9 to #3 
 
 Scaled Fargo AI assistant 4.1M to 27.5M users ; 17% savings in support costs 
 
 Led platform migration to API-first architecture - 35% faster data retrieval 
 
 Launched in-app marketplace (loans, deposits, wealth) - 23% conversion 
 
 Fintech AI Mobile Scale 
 
 Initiatives 
 
 
 
 
 📱 
 
 
 Teen Banking Platform 
 
 Led mobile strategy that grew app user base and jumped JD Power rankings 
 
 
 #9 to #3 
 
 
 
 
 Mobile Growth 
 
 
 
 The Problem 
 
 Wells Fargo's mobile app had strong download numbers but lagged in engagement and satisfaction. JD Power ranked it #9 among major banks. Users were downloading but not staying. 
 
 
 
 The Bet 
 
 Rather than adding features, we focused on reducing friction in the top 10 daily workflows. If the core experience felt faster and more intuitive, retention and satisfaction would follow. 
 
 
 
 What Shipped 
 
 Redesigned login, balance, and transfer flows. Reduced average task completion from 5 taps to 2. Added personalized quick actions on home screen. Performance optimization across Android and iOS. 
 
 
 
 The Outcome 
 
 Active users grew from 18M to 32M. JD Power ranking jumped from #9 to #3. App Store rating improved 0.6 points. Mobile became the primary channel for balance and transfer activity.

sections.heading: VP of Product, Mobile & AI Growth
sections.text: Wells Fargo - Strategy, Digital & AI · Nov 2023 - Oct 2025 Expanded consumer mobile app base 18M to 32M ; JD Power #9 to #3 Scaled Fargo AI assistant 4.1M to 27.5M users ; 17% savings in support costs Led platform migration to API-first architecture - 35% faster data retrieval Launched in-app marketplace (loans, deposits, wealth) - 23% conversion Fintech AI Mobile Scale Initiatives 📱 Teen Banking Platform Led mobile strategy that grew app user base and jumped JD Power rankings #9 to #3 Mobile Growth The Problem Wells Fargo's mobile app had strong download numbers but lagged in engagement and satisfaction. JD Power ranked it #9 among major banks. Users were downloading but not staying. The Bet Rather than adding features, we focused on reducing friction in the top 10 daily workflows. If the core experience felt faster and more intuitive, retention and satisfaction would follow. What Shipped Redesigned login, balance, and transfer flows. Reduced average task completion from 5 taps to 2. Added personalized quick actions on home screen. Performance optimization across Android and iOS. The Outcome Active users grew from 18M to 32M. JD Power ranking jumped from #9 to #3. App Store rating improved 0.6 points. Mobile became the primary channel for balance and transfer activity. Redesigned home screen with quick actions Streamlined transfer flow (2-tap) JD Power ranking progression 18M to 32M Active users #9 to #3 JD Power ranking 🤖 Fargo AI Assistant - Consumer Scale Scaled the bank's AI assistant from 4.1M to 27.5M active users 27.5M users Screenshot of the Fargo AI assistant in the Wells Fargo mobile app AI Scale Mobile The Problem Fargo, the bank's AI assistant, had been live for two years but sat at 4.1M users - a fraction of the mobile app base. Support call volume was rising, and the assistant wasn't absorbing the load it was designed to handle. Customers either didn't know it existed or tried it once and didn't come back. The Bet We believed the problem wasn't the AI itself - it was discovery and first-use experience. If we surfaced Fargo contextually (inside transactions, alerts, and search) rather than burying it in a menu, and invested in response quality for the top 50 intents, adoption would follow. What Shipped Contextual entry points across the mobile app (transaction disputes, balance inquiries, bill pay). Rebuilt the top 50 conversation flows with improved NLU. Added proactive nudges based on user behavior patterns. Integrated Fargo responses into push notification workflows. The Outcome Scaled from 4.1M to 27.5M active users in under 18 months. Support volume dropped 17%, saving an estimated $12M annually in call center costs. Fargo became the primary self-service channel for balance and transaction inquiries. Before: Fargo buried in hamburger menu After: Contextual entry in transaction view Rebuilt conversation flow for disputes Proactive nudge on push notification 4.1M to 27.5M Active users 17% Support volume reduction ~$12M Annual savings 18 mo Time to scale 🛒 In-App Marketplace for Banking Products Launched embedded marketplace for loans, deposits, and wealth products 23% conversion Fintech Growth Mobile The Problem Cross-sell happened almost entirely through branches and call centers. Mobile users had no way to discover or apply for new banking products in context. The app was a servicing tool, not a growth channel. The Bet If we surfaced relevant products at the moment of intent - a credit card offer after a large purchase, a savings rate after a deposit - conversion would beat traditional channels. The app needed to become a storefront, not just a dashboard. What Shipped Embedded marketplace within the mobile app for loans, deposits, credit cards, and wealth products. Contextual product recommendations powered by transaction data. Streamlined in-app application flows with pre-filled customer data. The Outcome 23% conversion rate on in-app product offers, significantly outperforming branch and call center channels. Mobile became a top-3 origination channel for consumer lending within the first year. Marketplace home screen Contextual product recommendation In-app application flow 23% Conversion rate Top 3 Origination channel 🎯 Engagement Toolkit for Feature Adoption Built a reusable system to drive discovery and adoption of new features +34% adoption Growth Mobile Platform The Problem The team was shipping features faster than users were finding them. New capabilities launched with minimal in-app awareness. Each product team was building its own one-off onboarding, creating inconsistent experiences and duplicated effort. The Bet A shared engagement toolkit - tooltips, spotlights, coachmarks, and in-app announcements - would give every product team a consistent way to introduce features. One investment, compounding returns across every launch. What Shipped Configurable engagement SDK with coachmarks, feature spotlights, progressive disclosure walkthroughs, and contextual tips. A/B testing framework for messaging variants. Analytics dashboard tracking feature discovery-to-adoption funnels. The Outcome 34% average increase in feature adoption for launches using the toolkit. Reduced time-to-first-use by 40% for new features. Adopted by 12 product teams across consumer mobile. Coachmark and spotlight examples Engagement SDK configuration Adoption funnel dashboard 34% Adoption lift 12 Teams using toolkit 🌐 Open Banking Initiative API-first architecture enabling third-party integrations and data sharing 35% faster Platform Transformation API The Problem Wells Fargo's data layer was built on legacy point-to-point integrations. Third-party fintechs were screen-scraping customer data because there was no sanctioned API. This created security risk, poor user experience, and an inability to participate in the open banking ecosystem. The Bet Migrating to an API-first architecture would eliminate screen scraping, improve data retrieval speed, and position the bank to participate in open banking partnerships rather than fight them. What Shipped RESTful API gateway for consumer data access. Token-based third-party authentication replacing screen scraping. Developer portal for partner onboarding. Internal API standardization across mobile, web, and partner channels. The Outcome 35% faster data retrieval across all channels. Eliminated screen scraping for 90%+ of third-party connections. Enabled new fintech partnerships that were previously impossible due to integration constraints. API gateway architecture Developer portal Partner integration flow 35% Faster retrieval 90%+ Scraping eliminated 👥 Segmentation and Personalization Personalized app experience based on customer segment and behavior +19% engagement AI Personalization Mobile The Problem 32 million users got the same app experience regardless of whether they were a college student with a checking account or a high-net-worth customer with a mortgage, brokerage, and credit line. One-size-fits-all was leaving value on the table. The Bet If we segmented users by lifecycle stage, product portfolio, and behavioral patterns, we could tailor the home screen, feature surfacing, and product recommendations to each customer's actual context. Relevance would drive engagement. What Shipped Customer segmentation engine using transaction history, product mix, and engagement signals. Dynamic home screen layouts per segment. Personalized quick actions and product recommendations. A/B testing framework for segment-level experiments. The Outcome 19% increase in daily active engagement. Personalized recommendations drove 2.3x higher click-through than generic placements. Laid the foundation for the AI-driven insights initiative that followed. Segment-specific home screens Personalized quick actions Segmentation logic flow 19% Engagement lift 2.3x CTR improvement 🎨 Visual Overhaul Modernized the mobile app's design system and visual language +0.6 rating Design Mobile UX The Problem The mobile app's visual design hadn't been meaningfully updated in years. It looked dated compared to competitors and fintech challengers. App Store reviews consistently cited the UI as feeling "old" and "clunky" even when functionality was strong. The Bet A comprehensive visual refresh - new typography, spacing, color system, and component library - would improve perceived quality without requiring major feature changes. Users judge capability by aesthetics whether we like it or not. What Shipped New design system with updated typography, color palette, and spacing. Modernized component library across 200+ screens. Phased rollout to minimize disruption. Accessibility improvements baked into every component. The Outcome App Store rating improved 0.6 points. "Design" and "look and feel" negative mentions in reviews dropped 45%. The updated design system reduced new screen development time by 30% through reusable components. Before and after comparison New component library Updated color and type system +0.6 App Store rating -45% Design complaints 📲 Device Innovation with Apple and Google First-mover integrations with new OS capabilities and hardware features Day-1 launches Mobile Innovation Partnerships The Problem Apple and Google were shipping new device capabilities every year - widgets, dynamic island, live activities, wallet passes - and Wells Fargo was consistently 6-12 months behind competitors in adopting them. Customers expected their banking app to keep pace with the platform. The Bet By embedding an engineering team in Apple and Google's early access programs, we could ship day-one support for new platform features. Being first would generate press, drive downloads, and signal to customers that Wells Fargo was a technology leader. What Shipped Day-one support for iOS widgets, Dynamic Island live activities for transactions, Android app shortcuts, and Wear OS/Apple Watch complications. Deep integration with platform wallet and payment APIs. Dedicated device innovation squad with Apple/Google liaison relationships. The Outcome Wells Fargo featured in Apple and Google keynote demos. Day-one launches became the standard rather than the exception. Widget and live activity adoption drove measurable increases in daily app opens. iOS widget on home screen Dynamic Island transaction view Apple Watch complication Day 1 Platform launches Featured Keynote demos 🔔 Mobile Push, Alerts and Badging Rebuilt the notification stack to drive re-engagement and reduce fraud response time +28% opens Mobile Engagement Growth The Problem Push notifications were treated as an afterthought - generic, poorly timed, and high volume. Customers were disabling notifications entirely, which meant they missed fraud alerts, payment confirmations, and time-sensitive actions. Opt-out rates were climbing. The Bet Fewer, smarter notifications would outperform the blast approach. If we made every push actionable and contextually relevant - and gave users granular control over what they received - they'd keep notifications on and engage with them. What Shipped Rebuilt notification infrastructure with priority tiers (critical, transactional, promotional). Rich push with inline actions (approve, dispute, snooze). Granular notification preferences per category. Smart badging that clears contextually. Fraud alert fast-path with one-tap response. The Outcome 28% increase in push-driven app opens. Notification opt-out rate dropped 15%. Fraud alert response time improved from hours to minutes. Rich push actions reduced friction for common tasks by 60%. Rich push with inline actions Notification preference center Fraud alert fast-path 28% App opens via push -15% Opt-out rate 📊 AI-Driven Customer Insights ML-powered spending insights and financial health signals surfaced in-app 3.1x engagement AI Personalization Mobile The Problem Customers had access to raw transaction data but no intelligence on top of it. They couldn't easily answer questions like "am I spending more on dining this month?" or "is this subscription charge new?" without manually scrolling through statements. The Bet If we applied ML to transaction data and surfaced proactive insights - spending trends, unusual charges, recurring payment changes, savings opportunities - the app would shift from reactive record-keeping to proactive financial guidance. What Shipped AI-powered spending categorization and trend analysis. Proactive alerts for unusual charges, subscription changes, and spending spikes. Monthly financial health summary with personalized recommendations. Integration with Fargo assistant for conversational exploration of insights. The Outcome Users who engaged with insights showed 3.1x higher session frequency. Unusual charge alerts prevented an estimated $8M in disputed transactions annually. Insights became the second most-used feature after balance check. Spending trend visualization Unusual charge alert Monthly financial health summary 3.1x Session frequency ~$8M Disputes prevented Director, Product Management Manager - Payments & Digital Channels

sections.heading: Director, Product Management Manager - Payments & Digital Channels
sections.text: First Republic · Apr 2016 - Oct 2023 Directed 22 PMs and $20M portfolio modernizing digital payments, lending, wealth Launched Zelle for 1M clients and Apple Pay - 27% mobile volume increase Led end-to-end platform migration (Fiserv to FIS) - 18% YoY revenue growth Deployed AI compliance engine - 90% accuracy gain Fintech Transformation Scale Payments Lending Initiatives 💳 Zelle P2P Payments Launched real-time P2P payments for 1M+ high-net-worth clients +40% over forecast Payments Mobile The Problem First Republic's clients had no way to send real-time peer-to-peer payments through the bank's digital channels. They were using Venmo and third-party apps, moving money outside the bank's ecosystem. Competitors had already launched Zelle months earlier. The Bet High-net-worth clients would adopt Zelle quickly if it was integrated natively into the mobile and web experience they already used daily. Speed to market mattered more than building a custom solution. What Shipped Zelle integration across mobile and web for 1M+ clients. Seamless enrollment from within the app. Real-time send and receive with instant notifications. Fraud detection rules tailored to high-value transactions. The Outcome Zelle adoption exceeded projections by 40% in the first quarter. P2P transaction volume grew steadily month over month. Kept client payment activity within the bank's ecosystem. Zelle enrollment flow Send money experience Transaction confirmation 40% Over forecast 1M+ Clients enrolled 📱 Apple Pay Contactless payments with same-day card provisioning for debit and credit Fastest adoption Payments Mobile Partnerships The Problem Clients were asking for contactless payment support and First Republic had no Apple Pay integration. The bank's card provisioning process was slow, requiring days to add cards to digital wallets. Competitors offered instant provisioning. The Bet Same-day card provisioning would differentiate First Republic's Apple Pay launch. If clients could add their card to Apple Wallet instantly from within the app, adoption would be significantly higher than industry averages. What Shipped Apple Pay support for debit and credit cards. Same-day in-app provisioning with one-tap activation. Push notifications for contactless transactions. Integration with Apple's Wallet API for real-time card status. The Outcome Apple Pay became the fastest-adopted feature in the app's history. Contactless transaction volume grew 27% within the first quarter. Client satisfaction scores for payments improved measurably. In-app card provisioning Apple Wallet integration Contactless payment confirmation 27% Volume increase #1 Fastest adopted feature 🔄 Core Platform Migration Led end-to-end migration from Fiserv to FIS across digital channels +18% revenue Transformation Platform The Problem The existing Fiserv core was limiting the bank's ability to launch new digital products. Integration complexity was adding months to every initiative. Revenue growth was flatlining. The Bet A full platform migration to FIS would be painful upfront but would unlock the product velocity needed to compete. The key was migrating without disrupting existing clients. What Shipped Phased migration of digital channels from Fiserv to FIS. Zero-downtime cutover strategy. Rebuilt integration layer for payments, lending, and wealth management. The Outcome 18% YoY revenue growth post-migration. New product launch cycle dropped from 6 months to 6 weeks. Zero client-impacting incidents during cutover. Migration architecture diagram Phased cutover timeline Post-migration metrics dashboard 18% YoY revenue growth 6mo to 6wk Launch cycle 📈 Eagle Tracker Portfolio Management Tool Built portfolio tracking and analytics tool for wealth management clients Real-time views Wealth 0 to 1 Mobile The Problem Wealth management clients had no self-service way to view portfolio performance, asset allocation, or gain/loss data digitally. They relied on quarterly statements and phone calls with their relationship manager for basic portfolio questions. The Bet A real-time portfolio management tool embedded in the mobile app would reduce RM call volume for routine inquiries and give clients the transparency they expected from a modern wealth platform. What Shipped Eagle Tracker: real-time portfolio dashboard with asset allocation views, performance charts, gain/loss tracking, and holdings detail. Integrated across mobile and web. Customizable watchlists and alerts for portfolio thresholds. The Outcome Significantly reduced routine portfolio inquiry calls to relationship managers. Clients gained real-time visibility into their investments for the first time. Became a key differentiator in wealth client retention conversations. Portfolio dashboard overview Asset allocation breakdown Performance chart views Real-time Portfolio visibility Reduced RM inquiry calls 💼 Digital Wealth Management Platform Launched end-to-end digital wealth platform for clients and advisors Full platform Wealth Platform 0 to 1 The Problem First Republic's wealth management experience was almost entirely offline. Account opening, document collection, investment preferences, and advisor communication all happened through paper, email, and phone calls. The experience didn't match the bank's premium brand. The Bet A comprehensive digital wealth platform - covering onboarding, document management, advisor messaging, and investment tracking - would modernize the client experience and free advisors to focus on high-value conversations instead of paperwork. What Shipped Digital wealth onboarding with e-signature and document upload. Secure advisor-client messaging within the app. Investment preference questionnaires and risk profiling. Consolidated wealth dashboard alongside banking accounts. The Outcome Reduced wealth onboarding time significantly. Advisor capacity increased as routine tasks moved digital. Client satisfaction scores for wealth services improved. Platform became a competitive differentiator for client acquisition. Wealth onboarding flow Advisor messaging interface Consolidated wealth dashboard Digital End-to-end onboarding Increased Advisor capacity 🛡 Stealth Mode on Mobile for Privacy Privacy mode that hides balances and sensitive data in public settings Client favorite Privacy Mobile UX The Problem High-net-worth clients were uncomfortable checking balances or reviewing transactions in public - airports, offices, coffee shops. With large balances visible on screen, the app felt risky to use outside of private settings. Some clients avoided the app entirely when in public. The Bet A one-tap "stealth mode" that masked balances, account numbers, and transaction amounts would make clients feel safe using the app anywhere. Privacy would drive frequency. What Shipped Stealth mode toggle that instantly masks all balances, account numbers, and transaction amounts with blur or placeholder values. Persists across sessions until toggled off. Quick-reveal with biometric tap for individual values. Configurable per account. The Outcome Became one of the most talked-about features among the client base. Increased mobile session frequency among high-balance clients. Generated positive press coverage as a privacy-first innovation in banking. Normal view vs stealth mode Quick-reveal biometric tap Stealth mode settings Increased Session frequency Press Industry coverage 📄 DOC AI - Data Extraction for Compliance AI-powered document processing to automate compliance data extraction 85% automation AI Compliance Automation The Problem Compliance teams were manually reviewing and extracting data from thousands of documents - tax forms, identification, financial statements, legal filings. The process was slow, error-prone, and couldn't scale with growing regulatory requirements. The Bet AI-powered document extraction (OCR + NLP) could automate the bulk of data capture from structured and semi-structured documents, reducing manual review to exception handling only. What Shipped DOC AI engine for automated extraction from tax documents, IDs, financial statements, and legal filings. Confidence scoring with human-in-the-loop review for low-confidence extractions. Integration with compliance workflow systems. Support for 50+ document types. The Outcome 85% of document data extraction fully automated. Processing time per document dropped from minutes to seconds. Error rate lower than manual review. Freed compliance staff to focus on judgment-based reviews. The process took days of scheduling and was especially frustrating for busy professionals and clients in other cities. The Bet A fully digital closing experience - document review, e-signature, notarization, and funding confirmation all within the app - would dramatically reduce closing times and remove the biggest friction point in the lending experience. What Shipped Digital closing application with secure document delivery, in-app review with annotation, e-signature integration, remote online notarization support, and real-time closing status tracking. Automated document assembly from loan origination data. The Outcome Closing time reduced from days to hours. Eliminated the need for in-branch visits for most loan types. Client satisfaction for the lending experience improved significantly. Operational cost per closing dropped substantially. Document review interface E-signature flow Closing status tracker Days to hrs Closing time Eliminated Branch visits 🖥 Enhancements to Banker Platform Modernized internal tools for relationship managers and branch staff +30% efficiency Platform Internal Tools The Problem Relationship managers were juggling multiple legacy systems to serve clients - one for accounts, another for loans, a third for wealth. Context-switching between tools during client conversations was slow and created a fragmented experience for both bankers and clients. The Bet A unified banker platform with a single client view - combining accounts, loans, wealth, and communication history - would reduce time-per-interaction and let RMs focus on the relationship rather than the tooling. What Shipped Unified client 360 view for relationship managers. Consolidated account, loan, and wealth data in a single interface. Quick-action workflows for common RM tasks. Client activity timeline showing digital and branch interactions. Integrated CRM notes and follow-up tracking. The Outcome 30% improvement in RM efficiency for client servicing tasks. Reduced average interaction time. Bankers reported significantly higher satisfaction with internal tools. Platform became the foundation for future internal product investments. sections.heading: AVP - Digital Product Manager (Auth and Mobile)
sections.text: Wells Fargo - Virtual Channels · Aug 2012 - Apr 2016 Led mobile-first innovation for payments and auth ( 18M users ) Built DailyChange app - 27% ACH growth Launched MFA - 40% less unauthorized access Integrated biometrics - 60% adoption, 25% login success gain Enabled cardless ATM - 30% fraud reduction Fintech Mobile 0 to 1 Security Initiatives 💰 DailyChange Gamified Payments App Gamified round-up savings app that increased ACH transfers by 27% +27% ACH 0 to 1 Payments Gamification The Problem Younger customers weren't engaging with traditional savings products. The bank needed a low-friction way to drive deposits and build savings habits among a demographic that wasn't setting up recurring transfers. The Bet A gamified "set it and forget it" round-up product - where every debit card purchase rounds to the nearest dollar and sweeps the difference to savings - would drive engagement without requiring behavior change. Adding streaks, milestones, and social nudges would make saving feel like a game, not a chore. What Shipped DailyChange: automatic round-up on debit transactions with gamification layer. Real-time savings tracker with streaks and milestones. Customizable multiplier (1x, 2x, 5x round-ups). Monthly savings summary with goal tracking and achievement badges. The Outcome 27% increase in ACH transfers. Average user saved $38/month without actively trying. The product became a key acquisition hook for younger demographics. Round-up transaction view Savings tracker with streaks Goal tracking & multiplier settings 27% ACH growth $38/mo Avg. savings 🔒 Enterprise Multi-Factor Authentication Enterprise rollout of MFA across online and mobile, securing 25M+ users -40% unauth access Security Scale Mobile The Problem Single-factor authentication left 25M+ active users vulnerable. Account takeover attempts were rising, regulatory pressure was increasing, and the bank had no consistent MFA solution across its online and mobile channels. The Bet A unified MFA rollout across both online and mobile - rather than channel-by-channel - would close the security gap faster and create a consistent experience. The key was doing it without tanking login completion rates. What Shipped Multi-factor authentication across online banking and mobile app for 25M+ active users. SMS, email, and authenticator app options. Adaptive step-up authentication based on risk signals. Seamless enrollment flows to minimize drop-off during migration. The Outcome Unauthorized access reduced by 40%. 25M+ active users secured with minimal impact on login completion rates. Established the authentication foundation that biometrics and device fingerprinting would later build on. MFA enrollment flow Step-up challenge screen Authentication method selection -40% Unauthorized access 25M+ Users secured 👤 Biometric Login Integration Touch ID and Android Fingerprint login driving 60%+ adoption 60%+ adoption Security Mobile UX The Problem Even with MFA in place, password-based login was still the primary entry point. Customers were forgetting passwords, getting locked out, and abandoning sessions. Login success rates were lower than they needed to be for an app serving millions. The Bet Integrating biometric authentication - Touch ID on iOS, Android Fingerprint on Android - would simultaneously improve security and reduce friction. Faster login would drive higher session frequency and fewer support calls. What Shipped Touch ID and Android Fingerprint integration for mobile login and transaction approval. Biometric enrollment during first login post-update. Fallback to passcode for failed biometric attempts. Support for both authentication and transaction signing. The Outcome 60%+ biometric adoption among mobile users. Login success rates improved by 25%. Password reset calls dropped significantly. Set the precedent for all future authentication investments at Wells Fargo. Touch ID login prompt Android Fingerprint integration Biometric enrollment flow 60%+ Biometric adoption +25% Login success rate 🔍 Device Fingerprinting & Behavioral Analytics Risk-based authentication using device signals and behavioral patterns -35% ATO Security AI Fraud The Problem Account takeover (ATO) attacks were getting more sophisticated. Stolen credentials could bypass MFA if attackers had access to a victim's phone or email. Static authentication rules weren't adapting to evolving threat patterns. The Bet Layering device fingerprinting and behavioral analytics on top of existing authentication would catch bad actors that credentials alone couldn't. If the device, location, or behavior pattern didn't match, step up the challenge - invisibly to legitimate users. What Shipped Device fingerprinting engine capturing hardware, OS, and browser signals. Behavioral analytics tracking login patterns, navigation habits, and session timing. Risk scoring engine that triggered adaptive step-up authentication. Continuous authentication signals throughout the session, not just at login. The Outcome 35% drop in account takeover attempts. Legitimate users experienced fewer challenges due to trusted device recognition. Fraud detection shifted from reactive to proactive. Established the risk-based authentication layer still in use today. Risk scoring dashboard Device trust signals Adaptive challenge flow -35% ATO attempts Fewer False challenges 🏧 Cardless ATM Access Mobile-authenticated cardless withdrawals across 12K+ ATMs -30% card fraud 0 to 1 Mobile Security The Problem Physical card fraud at ATMs - skimming, cloning, shoulder surfing - was a persistent and costly problem. Customers who lost or forgot their card had no way to access cash. The ATM experience hadn't evolved in decades. The Bet If customers could authenticate ATM withdrawals through their mobile app instead of a physical card, it would eliminate the skimming attack vector entirely and give customers a faster, more convenient way to access cash. What Shipped Cardless ATM access via mobile authentication across 12,000+ Wells Fargo ATMs. One-time access codes generated in the app. NFC tap-to-authenticate for supported devices. Biometric confirmation before code generation for added security. The Outcome Physical card fraud reduced by 30% at enabled ATMs. Expanded secure cash access across 12K+ ATMs nationwide. Customers used cardless access even when they had their card - the experience was simply better. Cardless ATM initiation in app One-time code generation NFC tap-to-authenticate -30% Card fraud 12K+ ATMs enabled 🔔 Alerts Next Gen Rebuilt the alerts platform to deliver smarter, real-time notifications across channels Next Gen ▾ Alerts Push Notifications Real-time Multi-channel Problem Legacy alerts system was batch-processed with limited customization. Customers received delayed or irrelevant notifications, leading to low engagement and missed fraud events. Bet A modern, event-driven alerts platform with granular customer preferences and real-time delivery would increase engagement and strengthen fraud detection. What Shipped Next-generation alerts engine with real-time event processing. Configurable alert preferences across push, SMS, and email. Smart bundling to reduce notification fatigue. Priority escalation for security-related alerts. Outcome Faster alert delivery, higher customer engagement with notifications, and improved fraud response times across the digital banking portfolio. Alert preferences dashboard Real-time notification flow Smart alert bundling Real-time Alert delivery Multi-channel Push, SMS, Email 🔄 Digital Redesign and Replatforming Led the redesign and migration of digital banking to a modern platform architecture Replatform ▾ Replatforming UX Redesign Architecture Migration Problem Legacy digital banking platform was built on aging infrastructure. Feature velocity was slow, the UX was dated, and the tech stack made it difficult to ship modern experiences at scale. Bet A full redesign paired with platform migration would unlock faster development cycles, improve the customer experience, and position the digital channel for future growth. What Shipped End-to-end digital banking redesign with modernized UI patterns. Platform migration from legacy monolith to a more modular architecture. Phased rollout to minimize customer disruption. Updated information architecture and navigation. Outcome Modernized digital banking experience with improved usability, faster feature delivery, and a scalable foundation for future product development. Before and after redesign New platform architecture Migration rollout plan Full UX redesign Modern Platform stack 🎓 Managed Student Loans Digital Roadmap Owned the digital product roadmap for the student lending portfolio Roadmap ▾ Student Loans Product Roadmap Digital Servicing Lending Problem Student loan servicing was primarily offline. Borrowers lacked self-service tools for payments, deferment, and account management, driving high call center volume and poor satisfaction. Bet A dedicated digital roadmap for student loans would reduce servicing costs, improve borrower satisfaction, and bring the lending experience in line with the rest of the digital banking portfolio. What Shipped Digital student loan dashboard with payment management, autopay enrollment, and deferment request flows. Self-service tools for loan details, payoff quotes, and document access. Integrated into the broader online and mobile banking experience. Outcome Improved borrower self-service rates, reduced call center volume for student loan inquiries, and a cohesive digital servicing experience for the lending portfolio. Student loan dashboard Payment management flow Self-service tools Self-serve Loan management Reduced Call center volume Senior Consultant - Tech Innovations

 Causes 

## Causes

 
 Organizations and missions I believe in 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Big Brothers Big Sisters 
 
 Creating and supporting one-to-one mentoring relationships that ignite the power and promise of youth. For over 100 years, they've matched adult volunteers with children ages 4 through 18 to help them reach their full potential. 
 
 Why I Care 
 
 Mentorship changed the trajectory of my own life. The idea that one consistent, caring adult can reshape a young person's future isn't abstract to me - it's something I've seen firsthand. Every kid deserves someone in their corner. 
 Donate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 St. Jude Children's Research Hospital 
 
 Advancing cures and means of prevention for pediatric catastrophic diseases through research and treatment. No child is denied treatment based on race, religion, or a family's ability to pay. 
 
 Why I Care 
 
 As a father of a thriving 4-year-old daughter, I'm reminded daily of what a blessing a healthy child is. Not every family gets that peace of mind. No parent should have to choose between their child's health and financial survival. St. Jude's model - where families never receive a bill - is what healthcare should look like. 
 Donate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 World Wildlife Fund 
 
 Conserving nature and reducing the most pressing threats to the diversity of life on Earth. Working in over 100 countries to build a future where people live in harmony with nature. 
 
 Why I Care 
 
 This was the first cause I ever got behind - as an 8-year-old in Dubai, I organized a cleanup-the-beach drive. It's also championed by my hero, Sir David Attenborough, whose work taught me to see the natural world differently. Today it's a cause my daughter and I share. We're borrowing this planet from the next generation, and WWF operates at the scale the problem demands. 
 Donate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Habitat for Humanity 
 
 Bringing people together to build homes, communities, and hope. Working across all 50 states and in approximately 70 countries to create a world where everyone has a decent place to live. 
 
 Why I Care 
 
 President Carter's decades of hands-on work with Habitat - well into his 90s, hammer in hand - is one of the most inspiring examples of service I've ever seen. A safe home isn't a luxury, it's the foundation everything else is built on. Habitat doesn't just build houses, they build the conditions for families to thrive. 
 Donate

 Learning & Certifications 

## Learning & Certifications

 
 Staying sharp across product, technology, and leadership 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 AI-Driven Product Strategy 
 
 Kellogg School of Management, Northwestern University 
 
 Issued Jun 2022 · Credential ID 53061978 
 
 AI Strategy Product Vision Go-to-Market 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 What I Learned 
 
 An eight-week executive program that integrates AI into every stage of the product lifecycle. Learned to use generative AI for opportunity analysis, stress-testing assumptions, simulating customer segments, and developing messaging. The biggest shift was moving from thinking about AI as a feature to treating it as a strategic lens across vision, discovery, roadmapping, and growth. 
 
 
 
 Skills Covered 
 
 Product Vision & Strategy AI-Powered Discovery Opportunity Analysis Go-to-Market Strategy Monetization & Pricing Product-Led Growth UX & Agile Development Stakeholder Alignment 
 
 
 
 
 Capstone 
 
 Developed AI-supported strategy artifacts across the full product lifecycle - from vision and opportunity analysis through GTM and monetization - using generative AI to simulate customer segments, stress-test assumptions, and build a defensible product roadmap. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Data Analytics 
 
 General Assembly 
 
 SQL Tableau Python 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 What I Learned 
 
 How to acquire, clean, analyze, and visualize data to drive real decisions. The bootcamp took a hands-on approach to turning raw datasets into clear narratives - from writing SQL queries and building Excel models to creating Tableau dashboards and Python scripts. Sharpened my ability to frame data as a story that informs product and business strategy. 
 
 
 
 Skills Covered 
 
 SQL Excel Tableau Power BI Python Data Cleaning Data Visualization AI Fundamentals 
 
 
 
 
 Capstone 
 
 Selected a real-world dataset, crafted a problem statement, and applied the full analytics workflow - data collection, cleaning, analysis, and visualization - to solve it. Prepared technical documentation and delivered a stakeholder presentation communicating findings and recommendations. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Certified ScrumMaster (CSM) 
 
 Scrum Alliance 
 
 Scrum Agile Facilitation 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 What I Learned 
 
 The difference between doing Scrum and being agile. The certification deepened my understanding of sprint mechanics, but the real value was learning how to remove blockers and create space for engineering teams to do their best work. Changed how I think about the PM's role in ceremonies. 
 
 
 
 Skills Covered 
 
 Scrum Framework Sprint Planning Retrospectives Backlog Management Team Facilitation Servant Leadership 
 
 
 
 
 Certification 
 
 Passed the CSM exam after a two-day intensive covering Scrum theory, roles, events, and artifacts. Applied the framework immediately to restructure sprint cadence on a cross-functional product team. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Business Strategy 
 
 McKinsey & Company 
 
 Issued Mar 2023 - View Credential 
 
 Strategy McKinsey Method Competitive Analysis 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 What I Learned 
 
 How to assess the robustness of a business strategy using McKinsey's Strategy Method. Covered frameworks for evaluating competitive positioning, identifying where to play and how to win, and stress-testing strategic assumptions. Sharpened my ability to connect product decisions to broader business strategy and communicate them in terms leadership teams actually care about. 
 
 
 
 Skills Covered

 MadLab / Chatbots & AI Assistants / Insurance AI Assistant 
 
 ▶ Quick reference 
 
 LangGraph — Framework for multi-step AI conversations. Tracks which stage you're in, what information it has, and what it needs next. 
 
 State Machine — A system in one of several defined states (greeting, collecting info, processing claim) that moves between them based on what happens. 
 
 NLP — Natural Language Processing. Lets software understand speech as people naturally talk, not just exact keyword matches. 
 
 Smart Escalation — When the AI hands off to a human with full conversation context. The agent sees everything — the user repeats nothing. 
 
 
 
 
 
 Live Prototype Chatbots & AI Assistants 
 Insurance AI Assistant 

## Insurance AI Assistant

 
 A chatbot that walks you through filing an insurance claim without losing track of anything. It remembers what you've said, asks the right follow-ups, and handles the whole process from start to finish. 
 
 LangGraph LangChain State Machine Python Flask NLP 
 
 Launch Prototype 
 
 
 
 
 The Problem 

## The Problem

 
 Insurance companies answer the same questions millions of times - filing claims, looking up policies, sorting out billing. Most chatbots make you start over every time you ask something new. You explain your situation, get handed off, and explain it all again. This prototype keeps track of the whole conversation so you never have to repeat yourself. 
 
 
 
 
 System Architecture 

## System Architecture

 
 Most chatbots follow a rigid script - if the conversation goes sideways, they're lost. This one uses LangGraph to manage the conversation in stages. Each stage knows what happened before it, so the bot can handle complex, multi-step requests without dropping the thread. 
 
 Why LangGraph over Dialogflow? Dialogflow is great for simple Q&A. But insurance claims aren't simple - they're multi-step, branching conversations. LangGraph remembers what you said three steps ago. Dialogflow doesn't.

Airbnb Mobile App Teardown: When Trust Breaks Down - Kiran Rao Skip to main content
