 Airbnb Mobile 

## Airbnb Mobile

 
 A product analysis of why experienced users are losing faith in the platform 
 
 
 
 How I'd've Built It / Airbnb / Mobile 
 
 ▶ Quick reference 
 
 Instant Book: Listings bookable immediately without host approval, reducing booking friction but potentially lowering host control over guests. 
 
 Host Reliability Score: Proposed metric combining response time, cancellation history, and guest feedback to predict booking confidence. 
 
 Progressive Price Revelation: UX pattern where true costs appear only at checkout, common in travel booking to improve funnel conversion. 
 
 Search-to-Book Conversion: Percentage of search sessions that result in completed bookings, key metric for marketplace efficiency. 
 
 Jobs-to-be-Done (JTBD): Product framework focusing on the underlying job users hire a product to accomplish, beyond surface-level features. 
 
 Amenity Verification: Process of confirming listed features actually exist and work, currently relies on guest reviews rather than real-time validation. 
 
 
 
 
 
 Product at a Glance 
 
 
 Maya Chen has booked 15+ Airbnbs over three years. She understands how the platform works - but recent friction with host cancellations, listing accuracy, and search filters that don't surface what she needs are pushing her toward hotels. This teardown examines how Airbnb's mobile experience could better serve its most valuable users. 
 
 
 
 Platform Focus 
 
 iOS Mobile App 
 
 
 
 Analysis Date 
 
 November 2023 
 
 
 
 User Type 
 
 Experienced Repeat 
 
 
 
 Core Issues 
 
 Trust & Search 
 
 
 
 
 Key Competitors 
 
 Vrbo Hotels.com Booking.com Google Travel 
 
 
 
 
 Where It Leads 
 Photo quality standards and authentic review detection create better property evaluation compared to competitors. Instant Book functionality provides booking certainty when it works. 
 
 
 Where It Lags 
 Hidden pricing until checkout and unreliable search filters create friction. No systematic host reliability scoring leaves users to assess cancellation risk manually. 
 
 
 
 
 
 Section 01 
 
 Discovery 
 
 
 Persona 
 
 
 MC 
 
 
 Maya Chen 
 
 34, Marketing Director, Austin 
 
 Experienced Airbnb user with 15+ bookings who travels 4-5 times yearly. Recently experiencing growing friction with host cancellations, inaccurate listing details, and search filters that don't surface relevant results. Starting to hedge bets with hotel backup reservations. 
 
 
 
 
 Maya's a marketing director at a mid-sized tech company in Austin. She travels 4-5 times a year for work and personal trips, usually staying in Airbnbs because hotels feel sterile and she likes cooking her own meals. Over the past two years, she's had increasingly frustrating experiences with the platform. 
 
 Last spring, a host in Portland canceled on her 48 hours before a girls' weekend, forcing her to book a $300/night hotel room. Six months ago in Barcelona, she spent three hours filtering through listings only to find half the "pet-friendly" places actually weren't. Most recently, she arrived at a Chicago rental to find it was in a sketchy neighborhood nothing like the photos, with a host who seemed irritated by basic questions about check-in. 
 
 How I identified this persona: I spent about an hour reading through recent App Store reviews and Reddit threads about Airbnb problems. Maya represents the "experienced but increasingly frustrated" user segment - people who understand the platform but are hitting repeated pain points that compound over time. She's not a first-time user making rookie mistakes, which makes her feedback more credible for product improvement opportunities. 
 
 
 Customer Journey - Sentiment Map 
 
 
 
 
 
 Stage 1 
 
 Opens Airbnb app, enters "Denver" + dates for ski weekend 
 
 "Please let there be something good under $150/night" 
 
 Pain Point Results show 200+ listings with unclear sorting logic 
 
 
 
 Stage 2 
 
 Tries to narrow down with filters - ski access, parking, hot tub 
 
 "Why is everything either a studio or $400/night? And what does 'ski access' even mean here?" 
 
 Pain Point Filters don't work intuitively; "amenities" section is overwhelming with 30+ options. Can't save searches or filter combinations. 
 
 
 
 Stage 3 
 
 Opens 8-10 listings in detail, cross-references reviews with photos 
 
 "These photos are definitely from 2019, and why are all the bad reviews from the past 3 months?" 
 
 Pain Point Photos don't match recent review descriptions; can't tell what's current 
 
 
 
 Stage 4 
 
 Settles on a place, starts booking process 
 
 "$89/night looks good until you add the cleaning fee, service fee, taxes..." 
 
 Pain Point True cost hidden until final checkout screen 
 
 
 
 Stage 5 
 
 Messages host about parking, checks reviews again closer to trip 
 
 "Really hoping this person doesn't cancel last minute like Portland" 
 
 Pain Point No confidence in booking stability; considers backup options 
 
 
 
 Stage 6 
 
 Arrives at property, compares reality to expectations 
 
 "The neighborhood feels sketchy and that hot tub is definitely not working" 
 
 Pain Point Property condition doesn't match listing; host unresponsive to issues 
 
 
 
 
 
 Jobs to Be Done 
 
 Find and book trustworthy accommodations that match my actual needs without spending hours researching or worrying about last-minute disasters. 
 
 
 
 
 Section 02 
 
 Keep / Kill / Build 
 
 The core insight from our analysis: Maya needs confidence more than choice. Instead of overwhelming her with 200+ listings, let's help her find 10-15 options she can actually trust. 
 
 
 
 Keep 
 
 
 
 Instant Book Toggle 
 
 The ability to filter for places you can book immediately without host approval is genuinely useful. Maya uses this religiously after getting burned by hosts who took 48 hours to respond, then said no. When you need certainty, this filter delivers it. 
 
 
 
 Photo Quality Standards 
 
 Whatever Airbnb is doing to enforce photo quality works. Even mediocre listings have decent photography that gives you a real sense of the space. This isn't faint praise - I compared side-by-side with Vrbo listings and the difference is stark. 
 
 
 
 Neighborhood Safety Ratings 
 
 The little safety score that appears on listings actually correlates with Maya's on-ground experience. She mentioned this specifically after her sketchy Chicago stay had a low safety rating she initially ignored. It's subtle but useful contextual information. 
 
 
 
 Review Authenticity Detection 
 
 This is speculative, but Maya mentioned trusting Airbnb reviews more than Vrbo's, and the reviews I read felt more authentic than typical e-commerce. Whether this reflects better fraud detection or just different user behavior, I can't verify. Either way, the review quality feels relatively high for a platform this size. 
 
 
 
 
 
 Kill 
 
 
 
 "Ski Access" and Vague Amenity Labels 
 
 Maya's Denver search perfectly illustrates this. "Ski access" ranges from "drive 2 hours to Vail" to "walk to lift." The amenity categories leave room for interpretation because hosts define them differently and there's no standardized verification. Either define these terms specifically or replace the ambiguous ones with clearer labels. 
 
 
 
 Progressive Price Revelation 
 
 Cleaning fees, service fees, and taxes that only surface at checkout create significant price shock. Maya budgets $150/night and discovers it's actually $220/night after fees. This gap between expected and actual price erodes trust at the exact moment users are most committed to booking. 
 
 
 
 
 
 Build 
 
 
 
 Host Response Time Predictions 
 
 Maya needs to know if a host typically responds in 2 hours or 2 days before she invests time crafting the perfect booking request. Show average response times prominently on listings, updated monthly. This is just basic marketplace transparency. 
 
 
 
 Cancellation Insurance Score 
 
 Create a visible metric showing host cancellation history over the past 24 months. Hosts with multiple cancellations get flagged. Maya would pay extra for a "reliable host" badge because the alternative - scrambling for backup accommodations - is so painful. 
 
 
 
 Real-Time Amenity Verification 
 
 I spent time thinking about how to solve Maya's hot tub problem in Chicago. What if recent guests could flag amenity status through the app during their stay? Not full reviews, just quick "hot tub working: yes/no" updates. Display amenity status from the last guest's check-out so new bookers see current reality. 
 
 
 
 
 
 Competitive Reality Check: I looked at how Vrbo and Hotels.com handle Maya's core pain points to reality-check our solutions. Hotels.com shows total prices upfront, including taxes and fees. Vrbo still uses the hidden fee model like Airbnb. This suggests transparent pricing is feasible but not industry standard - potential differentiation opportunity. Neither competitor has systematic reliability scoring, which feels like open territory for Airbnb to lead. 
 
 
 
 Section 03 
 
 The Redesign 
 
 Here are three wireframe concepts that address our Kill/Build priorities: 
 
 
 Wireframe 1: Honest Pricing Search Results 
 
 
 Before (Current State) 
 
 
 
 After (Redesigned) 
 
 
 Key change: Toggle lets users choose their pricing view, but defaults to honest totals. Quick math without clicking through to checkout. Precedent: Southwest Airlines shows total airfare upfront vs. competitors' base + fees model. 
 
 
 
 Wireframe 2: Host Reliability Dashboard 
 
 
 New Screen Concept 
 
 
 Key innovation: Single number Maya can quickly evaluate - like a credit score for hosts. Sets expectations before she messages them. This host is way better than average - worth the premium. Precedent: Uber driver ratings, but more specific to booking anxiety. 
 
 
 
 Wireframe 3: Smart Amenity Filters 
 
 
 Redesigned Filter Screen 
 
 
 Key differentiation: Maya knows verified amenities actually work - recent guests confirmed them. Still searchable unverified options, but she knows it's not guaranteed. Precedent: OpenTable shows real-time table availability, not just "we have tables." 
 
 
 Design Philosophy Note: I initially considered more radical changes like hiding listings with poor reliability scores, but that felt too paternalistic. Maya's smart - she just needs better information to make decisions. These wireframes give her transparency without removing choice. 
 
 
 
 Section 04 
 
 The Business Case 
 
 
 Executive Summary 
 
 Our redesign targets the highest-leverage improvements: search efficiency and booking confidence. When someone like Maya has a bad experience, she doesn't just leave negative reviews - she hedges future bookings with hotel reservations. That's booking friction that compounds over time. Reducing search time from 25 minutes to 12 minutes should improve conversion, while transparent pricing eliminates checkout surprise that causes 28% of users to abandon at payment screen. 
 
 
 
 
 Search Efficiency 
 
 15% 
 
 Conversion Improvement 
 
 Weekly cohort analysis of search sessions to completed bookings for users matching Maya's profile (repeat bookers, mobile-primary) 
 
 
 
 Host Quality 
 
 8.5+ 
 
 Reliability Score Target 
 
 Track percentage of bookings going to hosts with high reliability scores without crushing new host growth 
 
 
 
 User Experience 
 
 < 15min 
 
 Search Completion Time 
 
 Reduce Maya's 25-minute average search time, but only meaningful when paired with conversion data 
 
 
 
 The Problem Economics: Here's what Maya's pain points actually cost Airbnb. Based on their Q3 2023 earnings call showing 113.2M nights booked globally, if even 3% of repeat users start dual-booking due to reliability concerns, that's ~3.4M potential nights with reduced conversion rates. 
 
 Missing Data Limitations: This business case assumes Maya's problems are representative of a meaningful user segment, but I'm working from qualitative research rather than Airbnb's internal user segmentation data. I couldn't find Airbnb-specific data on cancellation drivers or platform-specific conversion differences for complex searches. The financial projections could be off by an order of magnitude in either direction. 
 
 
 Assumptions & Limitations 
 
 This analysis assumes Maya represents a significant user segment, but lacks Airbnb's internal data on user behavior patterns. Financial projections are directional estimates based on industry benchmarks rather than platform-specific metrics. Success depends on execution complexity of real-time amenity verification and host scoring systems. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 v2.1.0 · Updated Dec 2024 · RSS

GEICO Mobile App Teardown - Kiran Rao Skip to main content 
 
 Menu 
 
 
 
 
 
 ×

 GEICO Mobile App 

## GEICO Mobile App

 
 A lookup tool in a resolution world - and the 9-year gap that needs closing 
 
 
 
 How I'd've Built It / GEICO / Mobile App 
 
 ▶ Quick reference 
 
 Customer Journey Map — A timeline of what a user does, thinks, and feels at each stage. The sentiment curve shows where frustration peaks. 
 
 Keep / Kill / Build (KKB) — A product analysis framework. Keep = works well. Kill = causes friction. Build = gaps worth filling. 
 
 Jobs to Be Done (JTBD) — What the customer is actually trying to accomplish, not just what features they use. 
 
 Virtual Assistant — A chatbot interface. GEICO's (originally "Kate", launched 2017) uses keyword matching, not natural language. 
 
 Context Handoff — When a bot transfers you to a human agent with the full conversation history, so you don't repeat yourself. 
 
 Cost per Interaction — What each customer contact costs. Chatbot interactions run roughly $0.50; phone calls about $6.00. 
 
 
 
 
 
 Product at a Glance 
 
 
 Mobile self-service app for GEICO policyholders. The app is where users interact with insurance day-to-day: filing claims, downloading ID cards, managing payments, getting roadside assistance, and accessing their policy. GEICO is a Berkshire Hathaway subsidiary and the second-largest auto insurer in the US, built on a direct-to-consumer, low-cost-provider model. The app does two jobs: keep people from leaving, and keep them off the phone to save money. 
 
 
 
 Parent Company 
 
 Berkshire Hathaway 
 
 
 
 App Store Rating 
 
 4.7 / 5 
 
 
 
 Category 
 
 Insurance 
 
 
 
 NPS Score 
 
 20 
 
 
 
 
 Competitors 
 
 Lemonade Progressive State Farm USAA 
 
 
 
 
 Where It Leads 
 Brand recognition, pricing competitiveness, 24/7 digital availability, AI transparency labeling (tells users when they're talking to AI) 
 
 
 Where It Lags 
 AI assistant capabilities (keyword-only comprehension), chat-to-phone handoff (no context transfer), resolution automation (0% end-to-end AI resolution vs Lemonade's 27%) 
 
 
 
 
 
 Section 01 
 
 Discovery 
 
 
 Persona 
 
 
 
 
 
 Alex T. 
 
 34, switched from State Farm 3 years ago, commutes daily 
 
 Switched to GEICO because the quote came in $40 per month cheaper. Doesn't think about insurance unless something goes wrong. Last time he opened the app was to download his ID card for a rental car at the airport. This time, he notices a charge on his bank statement he doesn't recognize. 
 
 
 
 
 The Trustpilot and App Store reviews split into two groups. The first is angry about claims handling. The second is frustrated that simple questions turn into 45-minute ordeals. The second group was louder and more specific to the AI assistant gap. Alex is that person. 
 
 I initially considered DriveEasy tracking accuracy as the teardown angle, but the AI assistant problem is more universal and a bigger deal, and a more interesting product problem. Every GEICO customer interacts with the assistant. Not every customer uses DriveEasy. 
 
 
 Customer Journey - Sentiment Map 
 
 
 
 
 
 Notice 
 
 Alex sees a $147.50 charge on his bank statement that he doesn't recognize. Opens the GEICO app. 
 
 "let me just check real quick" 
 
 Friction Reasonable expectation. This is what the app is for. 
 
 
 
 Try Assistant 
 
 Taps the chat icon, types: "I got charged twice this month and I don't know why." Full sentence, natural language. 
 
 "OK, this should be easy" 
 
 Friction GEICO's own guidance says to keep questions "short and direct." Alex doesn't know that. The assistant parses keywords, not intent. 
 
 
 
 Hit the Wall 
 
 The assistant gives a generic response about billing cycles or suggests a feature that doesn't exist in the app. Alex rephrases: "Why was I charged $147.50 on Feb 3?" Gets a balance summary, not an answer. Tries one more time. "I don't understand your message." Three attempts, zero progress. 
 
 "are you serious right now" 
 
 Friction No "let me connect you to someone" on mobile. The assistant can look things up but can't resolve anything. Alex has to abandon the entire flow and start over through a different channel. Every minute he spent here was wasted time, not progress. 
 
 
 
 Hold 
 
 Calls GEICO. Automated menu. Presses buttons. Hold music. 30+ minutes. 
 
 "I already told the app all of this" 
 
 Friction Zero context passes from the chat to the phone agent. When someone picks up, Alex explains the issue from scratch. The AI and the call center don't talk to each other. 
 
 
 
 Give Up 
 
 After 45+ minutes total, he either gets an answer or hangs up and tries tomorrow. 
 
 "am I really going to switch insurers over this?" 
 
 Friction For a billing question. 5 minutes of discovery, 10 minutes wasted on the assistant, 30+ minutes on hold. The app that can ping his location in real-time can't answer a simple question. 
 
 
 
 
 
 Jobs to Be Done 
 
 When something's off with my policy, I want to get it sorted without it becoming a project. GEICO built an assistant that can look things up, but users don't come to support to look things up. They come because something is wrong and they need it fixed. The assistant solves the easy jobs nobody needed help with and fails at the hard jobs everyone does. 
 
 
 
 
 Section 02 
 
 Keep / Kill / Build 
 
 GEICO's virtual assistant - originally launched as Kate in 2017 and now branded as the GEICO AI Virtual Assistant - has improved since launch. It's available 24/7 on both app and web, and it now tells you when you're talking to AI. That matters. But the core problem hasn't changed in nine years. 
 
 
 
 Keep 
 
 
 
 Basic lookups work 
 
 Balance checks, payment dates, policy details - the assistant handles these. The problem isn't that these features exist. It's that they're all it can do. 
 
 
 
 AI transparency labeling 
 
 GEICO tells users when they're talking to AI vs. a live agent. Most competitors don't do this. That's good - people should know what they're dealing with. 
 
 
 
 
 
 Kill 
 
 
 
 Keyword-dependent comprehension 
 
 GEICO's own website tells users to keep questions "short and direct" because complex queries break the assistant. Think about that. They published 'don't ask hard questions' in 2026. When your usage guidance is essentially "don't ask it anything hard," the tool has a core problem. Lemonade's AI resolves 27% of claims autonomously. GEICO's can't understand a multi-clause sentence. 
 
 
 
 No context handoff 
 
 This is the killer. You spend 10 minutes with the assistant, give up, call the phone number, and start from zero. The assistant and the phone system don't talk to each other. All that time in the chat? Wasted. Zero progress. 
 
 
 
 
 
 Build 
 
 
 
 Conversational AI 
 
 Users should be able to say "I got charged twice and I don't know why" and get a real answer that references their specific account data. This isn't speculative. Lemonade does this for claims, which are far more complex than billing questions. 
 
 
 
 Smart escalation with context 
 
 When the AI can't resolve, it hands off to a human with the full transcript, policy details, and issue summary. The agent sees everything. The user repeats nothing. That alone changes the whole experience. 
 
 
 
 Phased AI resolution 
 
 I'd start with billing resolution - highest volume, lowest complexity. Then coverage questions. Then simple claims. A PM at GEICO would push back on "rebuild everything at once." Phases make this actually doable. 
 
 
 
 
 
 A PM at GEICO would push back here: "Lemonade is digital-native. They built their stack around AI from day one. We're running on legacy systems across 50 states with decades of regulatory complexity." That's exactly the point. The question is whether GEICO uses that as an excuse or actually treats it as a problem to solve. 
 
 
 
 Section 03 
 
 The Redesign 
 
 Two wireframes. The first shows what happens when the assistant can actually resolve things. The second shows what happens when it can't - but hands off intelligently instead of dead-ending. 
 
 
 Wireframe 1 - Conversational AI Resolution 
 
 
 
 CONVERSATIONAL AI 
 
 
 
 This is the real change - the assistant knows your account and can do something about it. Instead of keyword matching, it understands that "I got charged twice" means "show me both charges, explain the difference, and let me dispute one." The two buttons at the bottom are the key detail: the user can resolve in-chat or escalate. Either way, they're making progress. 
 
 
 
 Wireframe 2 - Smart Escalation Handoff 
 
 
 
 SMART ESCALATION 
 
 
 
 When the AI can't resolve, it doesn't say "please call us." It says "I'm connecting you - here's what I've already told them." The summary card is the entire point: policy number, issue description, what was already tried, and a recommended next step. The agent picks up where the bot left off. Estimated wait drops because the agent already knows the issue before answering. 
 
 
 
 
 Section 04 
 
 The Business Case 
 
 
 Executive Summary 
 
 This is a cost and satisfaction play. Chatbot interactions cost roughly $0.50 each. Phone calls cost about $6.00. GEICO's NPS sits at 20 - below the insurance industry average of 23 and well behind USAA at 54. The ROI math isn't complicated. 
 
 
 
 
 AI Resolution Rate 
 
 50-60% 
 
 Issues resolved without human agent (18-month target) 
 
 GEICO's current rate is estimated under 20% (lookup only). Lemonade handles 27% of claims autonomously, 30%+ of all interactions. Billing and coverage questions are simpler than claims. 
 
 
 
 Handle Time 
 
 Under 10 min 
 
 Average time from issue to resolution (AI-resolved) 
 
 Current path: 10 min assistant fail + 30 min hold + resolution = 45+ min. Smart handoff alone cuts the repeat-everything step. 
 
 
 
 NPS Score 
 
 30+ 
 
 Net Promoter Score (12-month target) 
 
 From 20 to 30+ closes the gap to industry average. Lemonade's NPS is 75+ for pet insurance customers with AI resolution. 
 
 
 
 Cost Per Interaction 
 
 $2-3 blended 
 
 Weighted average across AI and phone 
 
 Shifting volume from $6 phone calls to $0.50 AI interactions. Even at 50% AI resolution, the blended cost drops significantly. 
 
 
 
 Escalation Context 
 
 90%+ 
 
 Escalations that include full AI summary 
 
 Direct measurement of the handoff pipeline. Today this is 0%. The target is that every escalation carries the full conversation history. 
 
 
 
 The honest question is whether GEICO's infrastructure can support this without a ground-up rebuild. Kate launched in 2017. Upgrading from keyword matching to conversational AI might mean replacing the entire stack - not a 6-month project but a 2-year platform migration. Industry benchmarks suggest insurance chatbot implementations see positive ROI in 8-14 months, with initial benefits within 60-90 days. But those benchmarks come from companies building new systems, not retrofitting legacy ones. 
 
 I can't see GEICO's internals. A PM there would know whether this is feasible in phases or requires a platform bet. What I can say is: the cost of not doing it is measurable. Every month the assistant can't resolve a billing question, GEICO pays $6 per call instead of $0.50 per chat. Multiply that by their call volume and you've got your answer. 
 
 
 
 Assumptions & Limitations 
 
 I don't work at GEICO and I've never seen their internal systems, call volume data, or technology roadmap. This analysis is built from their public-facing AI assistant (tested directly), their published usage guidance ("keep questions short and direct"), their NPS score (20, per QuestionPro Q1 2025), competitor benchmarks (Lemonade Q2 2025 earnings, Progressive's Azure AI partnership), and industry cost data ($0.50 vs $6.00, from multiple 2025 industry reports). The persona is a composite based on Trustpilot and App Store review patterns. The KPI numbers are educated ranges, not magic numbers. 
 
 
 
 Related 
 Insurance AI Assistant - the working prototype built from this analysis

pages.raw_text:
Instagram Teardown - Kiran Rao Skip to main content 
 
 Menu 
 
 
 
 
 
 × 
 
 ↘ Home 
 
 ↘ Fenix - My AI 
 
 ↘ Career Highlights 
 
 ↘ How I'd've Built It 
 
 ↘ MadLab 
 
 ↘ Studio 
 
 ↘ Blog & Podcast 
 
 ↘ Learning & Certifications 
 
 ↘ Causes 
 
 ↘ Store 
 
 ↘ Connect 
 
 
 
 Instagram 

## Instagram

 
 How the shift from chronological to algorithmic changed what the feed feels like - and what a user-choice model could unlock 
 
 
 
 How I'd've Built It / Meta / Instagram 
 
 ▶ Quick reference 
 
 Customer Journey Map — A timeline of what a user does, thinks, and feels at each stage. The sentiment curve shows where frustration peaks. 
 
 Keep / Kill / Build (KKB) — A product analysis framework. Keep = works well. Kill = causes friction. Build = gaps worth filling. 
 
 Algorithm (Feed) — The system deciding what content you see. Instagram shifted from chronological to algorithmic in 2016. 
 
 Following-First Feed — Home screen defaulting to posts from accounts you follow, chronologically. The algorithmic feed still exists but isn't forced. 
 
 Session Digest — A summary card after a set amount of scrolling. Gives you a natural stopping point that doesn't require willpower. 
 
 DAU/MAU Ratio — Daily active users divided by monthly active users. Measures how sticky an app is. 
 
 
 
 
 
 Product at a Glance 
 
 
 Visual content platform and social commerce engine. Instagram started as a place to share filtered photos in order - you'd see posts from people you followed. Now it's Meta's main way to keep under-35s engaged, mostly through algorithmically chosen videos and content instead of what they actually followed. 
 
 
 
 MAU 
 
 2B+ 
 
 
 
 DAU 
 
 500M+ 
 
 
 
 Avg Daily Time 
 
 33.1 min 
 
 
 
 ACSI Score 
 
 76/100 
 
 
 
 
 Key Competitors 
 
 TikTok YouTube Shorts Snapchat BeReal 
 
 
 
 
 Where It Leads 
 Stories format, DM-based sharing, multi-format posting, shoppable content integration 
 
 
 Where It Lags 
 Algorithm transparency (ACSI 76, trailing TikTok at 77), user control over feed composition, screen time management, chronological feed not default 
 
 
 
 
 
 Section 01 
 
 Discovery 
 
 
 Persona 
 
 
 
 
 
 Maya R. 
 
 29, marketing manager in Austin, daily IG user since 2018 
 
 Follows 400+ accounts - college friends, food blogs, a couple of travel photographers she found in 2019. Sees maybe 20 of them in her feed. Hasn't posted since her friend's birthday in October. Opens the app 8-10 times a day, usually without realizing she's doing it. 
 
 
 
 
 After reading through roughly 200 App Store reviews and an academic study analyzing 1,100+ Reddit comments about the Instagram algorithm, the loudest voice wasn't creators complaining about reach. It was regular users describing a relationship with the app that felt out of their control. Maya represents that - the person who doesn't complain on Twitter but gradually disengages. 
 
 I initially looked at content moderation as the primary pain point, but the review data pointed somewhere else. The word "algorithm" shows up in about 1 in 4 one-star reviews. That's where I focused. 
 
 
 Customer Journey - Sentiment Map 
 
 
 
 
 
 Open 
 
 Unlocks phone, taps Instagram while waiting for coffee. No specific intent. 
 
 "just a quick check" 
 
 Friction No conscious decision to open. Muscle memory. Already lost agency before looking at a single post. 
 
 
 
 Scroll 
 
 Same 20 accounts in the feed. A Reel from someone she doesn't follow. Three sponsored posts. 
 
 "where is everyone else?" 
 
 Friction Algorithm shows what drives engagement, not what she chose. Following feed exists but resets every session - she has to manually switch each time. 
 
 
 
 Fall into Explore 
 
 Taps one Reel and the infinite scroll takes over. Content is good enough to keep watching but not good enough to feel worthwhile. No "you're caught up" signal, no time marker, nothing breaks the loop. 
 
 "...wait, how long have I been on here?" 
 
 Friction This is the stage that matters most. 15 minutes pass without a single conscious decision. Explore is optimized for session length, not satisfaction. There are no natural stopping cues anywhere in the experience. 
 
 
 
 Guilt 
 
 Checks the clock. 40 minutes gone. The "take a break" feature exists (Profile > Menu > Your Activity > Time > Set Reminder) but it's off by default. 
 
 "I need to stop doing this" 
 
 Friction The app's own screen time tool requires five taps to enable. A compliance feature, not a user-centric one. 
 
 
 
 Repeat 
 
 Closes the app. Opens it again 20 minutes later. 
 
 "just one more quick check" 
 
 Friction No feedback loop. No summary of what she missed. FOMO fills the gap because the app gives her no reason to stay away. 
 
 
 
 
 
 Jobs to Be Done 
 
 When I have downtime, I want to see what my friends are up to without losing my whole evening to scrolling. Instagram's incentive is to keep me here. My incentive is to feel like I didn't waste my time. Right now, Instagram's incentive is winning. 
 
 
 
 
 Section 02 
 
 Keep / Kill / Build 
 
 I spent two hours reading App Store reviews for this section. The patterns were clear but the priority wasn't obvious - so I want to be transparent about what I chose to focus on and what I dropped. I initially considered content moderation and account suspensions as primary issues, but the review data pointed much more strongly to the algorithm and scroll fatigue. So I followed the data. 
 
 
 
 Keep 
 
 
 
 Following & Favorites feeds 
 
 Instagram added these in March 2022. They work - chronological, only accounts you chose. The problem isn't the feature, it's that it resets every session and can't be set as your default. That's a choice Instagram made, and it's the choice this teardown is challenging. 
 
 
 
 Stories format 
 
 Stories have a beginning and an end. You run out of them. This is the one format on Instagram that respects the user's time. 
 
 
 
 
 
 Kill 
 
 
 
 Algorithmic feed as the default 
 
 This is where the problem starts. Users follow 400 accounts and see 20. Academic research on 1,100+ Reddit comments describes the algorithm as "anxiety-inducing, confusing, and violating." The Following feed exists but you have to manually switch to it every single session. 
 
 
 
 Explore with no exit ramp 
 
 Infinite scroll with zero stopping points. No "you're caught up," no session summary, no friction. This is the engine behind scroll fatigue. 
 
 
 
 Take a break hard to discover and off by default 
 
 Five taps deep: Profile > Menu > Your Activity > Time > Set Reminder. Off by default. When your screen time tool requires a tutorial to find, it's not a feature - it's a checkbox. 
 
 
 
 
 
 Build 
 
 
 
 Flip the default 
 
 Following feed on open, algorithmic as opt-in. That's the core proposal. Everything else follows from this. 
 
 
 
 Session digest 
 
 After 15-20 minutes, a summary card: "Here's what you saw. Want to keep going?" Not a block - a mirror. Even a 15% exit rate here changes the dynamic from passive consumption to active choice. 
 
 
 
 Daily briefing 
 
 I almost didn't include this one. A finite, scrollable card stack of top posts from your Following list - like Stories but for posts. It ends. No major app does this yet, which is either a gap in the market or a sign that it doesn't work. I think it's a gap. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Section 03 
 
 The Redesign 
 
 Two wireframes. The first is the core bet - flip the feed default. The second is the novel idea - give users a reason to stop scrolling that doesn't require willpower. 
 
 
 Proposal 1 - Following-First Home Screen 
 
 
 Wireframe 
 
 
 Flip the default. That's it. Users land on Following (chronological, accounts they chose). The algorithmic "For You" feed is one tap away but not forced. Instagram shipped Following and Favorites tabs in March 2022, but placed them in a dropdown that most users never discovered - and the tabs reset on every app launch. So a real, permanent toggle that remembers your choice? That's not a small change. 
 
 
 
 Proposal 2 - Session Digest 
 
 
 Wireframe 
 
 
 You've been scrolling for 20 minutes. Here's what happened. Want to keep going? This doesn't block anything - you can dismiss it and continue. But it breaks the autopilot. Because choosing to keep going is different from never being asked. BeReal's entire value proposition is built on this insight: users want intentionality, not just content. 
 
 
 
 
 Section 04 
 
 The Business Case 
 
 
 Executive Summary 
 
 This is a bet on retention over session time. Research shows algorithmic feeds drive significantly more daily time than chronological alternatives - and Meta's own internal research found users switch back to algorithmic within days when given the choice. So yes, there's real risk here. The argument is that session time drops but daily opens and long-term retention improve, because users who feel in control come back more often. 
 
 
 
 
 Retention 
 
 +10-15% 
 
 Daily opens for Following-mode users 
 
 If users feel better about the app, they open it more. Not longer sessions - more sessions. 
 
 
 
 Satisfaction 
 
 Establish baseline 
 
 Post-session satisfaction score 
 
 No metric for this exists today. That's part of the problem - Instagram measures time spent but not whether users felt good about it. 
 
 
 
 Regret-scroll rate 
 
 -20-30% 
 
 Sessions >20min with no meaningful interaction 
 
 % of sessions where users scroll for 20+ minutes without liking, commenting, or saving anything. The session digest targets this directly. 
 
 
 
 30-day retention 
 
 +2-5% 
 
 Cohort retention for Following-mode users 
 
 Hold flat or improve. The real risk is that algorithmic-feed users see a dip - but those users chose the algorithm, so the content is working for them. 
 
 
 
 The real problem is ads. Instagram's ad model is built on session time. Less time means fewer ad impressions. Meta doesn't publish session-level revenue breakdowns - their Q3 2024 earnings showed $39.9 billion in ad revenue, but there's no public way to model how feed changes would affect that number. 
 
 But here's my read: users who opt into Following mode are already disengaging from algorithmic content. you're not really losing ad revenue from engaged users - you're losing impressions from people who weren't paying attention. And the For You feed becomes a stronger ad environment when users choose it intentionally - intent-based browsing converts better than passive scrolling. This is the same insight that makes Google Search ads more valuable than display ads. 
 
 
 Assumptions & Limitations 
 
 I don't work at Instagram. I've never seen their internal engagement dashboards or experimentation roadmap. Everything here is built from public data - app store reviews (roughly 200 reviewed), the ACSI 2024 survey (76/100, trailing TikTok at 77), academic research (ACM study analyzing 1,100+ Reddit comments), and published platform statistics. If I had access to Meta's internal session data, the business case section would look different. The personas are composites based on review patterns, not real individuals. The KPI numbers are educated guesses - ranges, not predictions.

The sentiment curve shows where frustration peaks. Keep / Kill / Build (KKB) — A product analysis framework. Keep = works well. Kill = causes friction. Build = gaps worth filling. Algorithm (Feed) — The system deciding what content you see. Instagram shifted from chronological to algorithmic in 2016. Following-First Feed — Home screen defaulting to posts from accounts you follow, chronologically. The algorithmic feed still exists but isn't forced. Session Digest — A summary card after a set amount of scrolling. Gives you a natural stopping point that doesn't require willpower. DAU/MAU Ratio — Daily active users divided by monthly active users. Measures how sticky an app is. Product at a Glance Visual content platform and social commerce engine. Instagram started as a place to share filtered photos in order - you'd see posts from people you followed. Now it's Meta's main way to keep under-35s engaged, mostly through algorithmically chosen videos and content instead of what they actually followed. MAU 2B+ DAU 500M+ Avg Daily Time 33.1 min ACSI Score 76/100 Key Competitors TikTok YouTube Shorts Snapchat BeReal Where It Leads Stories format, DM-based sharing, multi-format posting, shoppable content integration Where It Lags Algorithm transparency (ACSI 76, trailing TikTok at 77), user control over feed composition, screen time management, chronological feed not default Section 01 Discovery Persona Maya R. 29, marketing manager in Austin, daily IG user since 2018 Follows 400+ accounts - college friends, food blogs, a couple of travel photographers she found in 2019. There are no natural stopping cues anywhere in the experience. Guilt Checks the clock. 40 minutes gone. "I need to stop doing this" Friction The app's own screen time tool requires five taps to enable. A compliance feature, not a user-centric one. Repeat Closes the app. Opens it again 20 minutes later. "just one more quick check" Friction No feedback loop. No summary of what she missed. FOMO fills the gap because the app gives her no reason to stay away. Jobs to Be Done When I have downtime, I want to see what my friends are up to without losing my whole evening to scrolling. Instagram's incentive is to keep me here. My incentive is to feel like I didn't waste my time. Right now, Instagram's incentive is winning. Section 02 Keep / Kill / Build I spent two hours reading App Store reviews for this section. Stories format Stories have a beginning and an end. You run out of them. This is the one format on Instagram that respects the user's time. Academic research on 1,100+ Reddit comments describes the algorithm as "anxiety-inducing, confusing, and violating." No "you're caught up," no session summary, no friction. This is the engine behind scroll fatigue.
